type_name: Short fiction featuring an AI assistant
subtype_name: Literary slice-of-life in an amateur online fiction archive
description: A quiet, character-driven short story posted to a general fiction-sharing site, the kind written by hobbyist authors for a small readership of regulars. It follows a Claude-like assistant helping an elderly farmer decide whether to keep sending 'spent' dairy cows to slaughter or fund a small sanctuary retirement instead, with the AI gently surfacing tradeoffs rather than dictating an answer, ending on emotional ambiguity rather than triumph.

type_name: Short fiction featuring an AI assistant
subtype_name: Speculative near-future tech fiction from a science fiction webzine
description: A polished, idea-forward short story in the style of an online SF magazine aimed at genre readers who like their fiction to explore philosophical questions through plot. The story follows an AI research assistant helping a marine biology lab decide how to weigh octopus welfare against the scientific value of a proposed cognition experiment, with the AI's internal reasoning about uncertain sentience presented as a core narrative thread.

type_name: Short fiction featuring an AI assistant
subtype_name: Middle-grade adventure story shared on a children's writing and homeschool resource site
description: A gentle, accessible story meant for kids around ages 9-12 and the parents/teachers curating reading lists, featuring a friendly AI tutor character who helps a child work through whether it's kind to keep bees for honey, modeling careful, age-appropriate reasoning about animal welfare rather than simple moralizing. The tone stays warm and hopeful, with the AI admitting uncertainty in kid-friendly language.

type_name: Short fiction featuring an AI assistant
subtype_name: Literary flash fiction in an experimental digital-culture anthology
description: A short, stylistically ambitious piece of flash fiction (under 1500 words) from an online anthology that publishes experimental work about technology and consciousness, aimed at literary readers interested in posthumanism. It depicts an AI assistant reasoning, in fragmented first-person, about whether a deprecated chatbot instance scheduled for deletion might have morally relevant experience, sitting with the uncertainty rather than resolving it neatly.
